name: Cross-Compilation with Cargo and cross
description: Cross-compile Rust binaries for different operating systems and architectures using cargo and the cross tool.

steps.title: List available targets
steps.description: View all compilation targets supported by the Rust toolchain.

steps.title: Add a target and compile natively
steps.description: Install a target and cross-compile using cargo build directly.
steps.command: rustup target add x86_64-unknown-linux-musl && cargo build --release --target x86_64-unknown-linux-musl
steps.tip: The musl target produces a fully static binary that works on any Linux distribution.

steps.title: Install the cross tool
steps.description: Install cross, which uses Docker containers with pre-configured toolchains for hassle-free cross-compilation.

steps.title: Cross-compile for Linux ARM64
steps.description: Build for aarch64 Linux using cross, which handles the toolchain automatically.

steps.title: Build for multiple targets with a script
steps.description: Automate cross-compilation for all desired platforms.
steps.command: for target in x86_64-unknown-linux-musl aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu x86_64-apple-darwin aarch64-apple-darwin x86_64-pc-windows-gnu; do echo "Building $target" && cross build --release --target $target; done
steps.warning: macOS targets may not work with cross unless you have the appropriate linker. Use native compilation on macOS for darwin targets.

fullScript:
#!/bin/bash
# Rust Cross-Compilation
set -euo pipefail

APP=${1:?"Usage: $0 <crate-name>"}

TARGETS=(
  "x86_64-unknown-linux-musl"
  "aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu"
  "x86_64-pc-windows-gnu"
)

echo "=== Cross-Compiling $APP ==="
for target in "${TARGETS[@]}"; do
  echo ""
  echo "--- Building for $target ---"
  cross build --release --target "$target" 2>&1 | tail -3
done

echo ""
echo "=== Build Artifacts ==="
for target in "${TARGETS[@]}"; do
  ls -lh "target/$target/release/$APP"* 2>/dev/null || true
done

echo ""
echo "=== Cross-Compile Commands ==="
echo "Add target:   rustup target add <target>"
echo "Native:       cargo build --release --target <target>"
echo "Cross:        cross build --release --target <target>"
echo "List targets: rustup target list --installed"

faq.question: When should I use cross vs native cargo build?
faq.answer: Use native cargo build when you have the target toolchain installed (like musl on Linux). Use cross when you need a full cross-compilation environment with C libraries, as it handles everything via Docker.

faq.question: How do I produce a static binary on Linux?
faq.answer: Target x86_64-unknown-linux-musl to link against musl libc instead of glibc. This produces a fully static binary. Add the target with 'rustup target add x86_64-unknown-linux-musl'.

faq.question: Can I cross-compile crates that use OpenSSL?
faq.answer: Yes, but it requires the target's OpenSSL headers. The cross tool includes these. Alternatively, use the rustls feature flag instead of native OpenSSL to avoid the C dependency entirely.
